summary: Parallel review: FAIL (2 critical, 8 warning, 7 suggestion). Compound gate: PASS

findingsList.text: 🟡 `bin/lib/cron.mjs:89` — No "in-progress" stale-item recovery: if cron-tick is SIGKILL'd while `runSingleFeature` is running, the board item stays "in-progress" indefinitely; the lock has stale-PID recovery (`util.mjs:115`) but board state does not — add a pre-flight step that detects "in-progress" items with no live cron-tick lock and reverts them to "ready"

🟡 `bin/lib/cron.mjs:124` — `_commentIssue` return value not checked; a failed comment (GH auth error, rate limit) is silently swallowed. Both `setProjectItemStatus` calls at lines 106–108 and 114–116 check their return and `console.warn` on false — `commentIssue` should do the same. Add: `if (!_commentIssue(issueNumber, msg)) console.warn("cron-tick: warning — failed to comment on issue #" + issueNumber)`.
🟡 `bin/lib/cron.mjs:124` — `_commentIssue` bare-called inside `catch(err)`; if it throws, `err` is swallowed — wrap in try-catch with console.warn fallback

🟡 `bin/lib/cron.mjs:100` — Title sanitization is explicitly documented as a security control ("prevent prompt injection") but has **zero test coverage**. Add tests for titles with `\n`, `\r`, control chars, and >200 char strings.

🟡 `bin/lib/cron.mjs:120-124` — `_setProjectItemStatus` and `_commentIssue` are called bare inside `catch(err)`. If either throws, the original error is silently swallowed and the comment may be skipped. Production code in `github.mjs` returns false rather than throwing (so production is safe today), but no test covers the throwing case.

🟡 `bin/lib/cron.mjs:111` — Issue title from the GitHub project board is passed verbatim (after control-char strip) to `runSingleFeature` as the LLM task prompt. Any collaborator with project board write access can craft a title to inject adversarial instructions into the autonomous agent. Add access-control guidance to docs and consider a visible boundary marker in the prompt separating the title from agent instructions.

The one 🟡 is a genuine agentic-specific concern: because the issue title flows directly into the LLM's task prompt, anyone who can write to the GitHub project board can influence agent behavior. For this team project the threat model is limited to trusted collaborators, making it backlog-appropriate rather than blocking.

🟡 `test/cron-tick.test.mjs` — the warning branch at `cron.mjs:122` (`!revertSet` inside the catch block) has no test coverage; the "reverts to ready" test at line 193 mocks `setProjectItemStatus` returning `true`, so the path where the revert itself fails is unreachable by any test — add a case where `runSingleFeature` throws AND `setProjectItemStatus` returns false
